1:
On Revelation 4:1 — “After this I looked, and, behold, a door was opened in heaven… and the first voice… said, Come up hither, and I will shew thee things which must be hereafter” — the witnesses mark the opening of the heavenly vision.

The Latin fathers read the door spiritually. Victorinus: “the new testament is announced as an open door in heaven.” The grammatical-historical Robertson: “after these things” (meta tauta), change in the panorama, not chronology; this vision is of heaven. Ellicott: he saw, and lo! the door already set open. Wesley: shown after a heavenly manner whatever should be.

The verse opens the second great vision. A door is opened in heaven and John is called up to see things to come. The scene shifts from earth to the throne of God.

So the witnesses see John, the letters to the churches finished, caught up into a vision of heaven itself. The witnesses note the transition: “after this” (or “after these things”) — marking, as Robertson observes, a change in the panorama of visions rather than a strict sequence of events; the first vision (of Christ among the lampstands and the messages to the seven churches) gives way to a new and heavenly scene. They mark the open door: “a door was opened in heaven” — an aperture into the unseen world, granting John sight of the celestial throne-room; Victorinus reads the open door spiritually as the new covenant’s access to heaven. They observe the summoning voice: the first voice, like a trumpet, that had spoken at the outset, now says “Come up hither” — calling the seer up to the higher vantage of heaven. They note the purpose: “I will shew thee things which must be hereafter” — what God has ordained to come to pass is to be unveiled; the rest of the book flows from this. They observe that John is shown these future things “after a heavenly manner,” in symbol and vision, from the standpoint of the throne. They note the fitness of beginning the revelation of the future with a vision of God enthroned: history is unveiled only after its Sovereign is seen. The verse thus opens the great vision: the door in heaven, the summoning voice, and the promise of things to come — into which, the next verse says, John is at once caught in the Spirit.
2:
On Revelation 4:2 — “And immediately I was in the spirit: and, behold, a throne was set in heaven, and one sat on the throne” — the witnesses mark the central throne and its Occupant.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the rapture. Robertson: “straightway I was in the Spirit” (egenomen en pneumati), as in Revelation 1:10, perhaps a higher degree. Barnes: at once absorbed in the contemplation of the vision. Wesley: he is shown how whatever should be is transacted in heaven before the throne.

The verse sets the throne at the centre. John, in the Spirit, sees a throne in heaven and One seated on it. God enthroned governs all that follows.

So the witnesses see the vision’s first and central object: a throne, and One seated upon it. The witnesses note the rapture: “immediately I was in the spirit” — John is again caught into the prophetic ecstasy (as at the first vision), perhaps, Wesley suggests, in a higher degree, his soul wholly absorbed in the heavenly sight. They mark the dominating object: “a throne was set in heaven” — the first thing seen, fixed and established, the seat of universal sovereignty; all else in the vision is arranged around it. They observe the reverent reticence concerning the Occupant: “and one sat on the throne” — God himself, but unnamed and undescribed in form; the seer says only that One sat there, the very vagueness guarding the divine majesty against any image. They note that the throne is the governing symbol of the whole book: before the future is unveiled, the sovereignty of God over all is established; history will be shown to proceed from this throne. They observe that the One enthroned is the assurance behind every coming judgment and deliverance: nothing happens but from the throne. They note that to see God reigning is the necessary foundation for all that follows. The verse thus fixes the centre of the vision: a throne set in heaven, and One seated upon it — whose appearance, like precious stones, the next verse describes.

So the witnesses see the vision’s first and central object: a throne, and One seated upon it. The rapture “immediately I was in the spirit” marks John caught again into the prophetic ecstasy (as at the first vision), perhaps in a higher degree, his soul wholly absorbed in the heavenly sight. The dominating object “a throne was set in heaven” is the first thing seen, fixed and established, the seat of universal sovereignty, around which all else is arranged. The reverent reticence concerning the Occupant — “one sat on the throne” — names God himself, unnamed and undescribed in form, the very vagueness guarding the divine majesty. Before the future is unveiled, the sovereignty of God over all is established; nothing happens but from the throne.
3:
On Revelation 4:3 — “And he that sat was to look upon like a jasper and a sardine stone: and there was a rainbow round about the throne, in sight like unto an emerald” — the witnesses mark the glory of the enthroned God.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the symbols. Robertson and Barnes: the jasper, a brilliant translucent stone (cf. Revelation 21:11), the sardine fiery red; not a form but a splendour. Adam Clarke: the description points to surrounding glory, not the person. Ellicott: guided by Bible imagery and the nature of the stones. Wesley: a lustre like sparkling precious stones, as on the high priest’s breastplate.

The verse describes the divine splendour. The Enthroned shone like jasper and sardine, with an emerald rainbow round the throne. Light and the covenant bow surround God’s majesty.

So the witnesses see the enthroned God described not in form but in radiant splendour. The witnesses note the reticence the expositors stress: no shape or figure of God is given; only his dazzling appearance, lest any likeness be made of the invisible. They mark the gems: “like a jasper and a sardine stone” — the jasper a brilliant, clear, light-flashing stone, the sardine a deep fiery red; together they image the blended glory and, some suggest, the holiness and justice, the purity and the consuming fire, of God; Wesley likens the lustre to the jewels on the high priest’s breastplate. They observe the encircling bow: “a rainbow round about the throne, in sight like unto an emerald” — a complete circle of rainbow, of a soothing green emerald hue, surrounding the throne. They note the rich significance the expositors find in it: the rainbow is the ancient sign of God’s covenant and mercy (given to Noah), so that the throne of awful majesty is ringed with the token of grace; justice and judgment proceed from a throne girded with covenant faithfulness. They observe that the green of the emerald tempers the fiery brilliance, mercy softening majesty. They note that the whole picture holds together splendour, holiness, and covenant mercy. The verse thus arrays the enthroned God in glory: flashing like jasper and sardine, encircled by the emerald rainbow of covenant mercy — the central majesty around which, the next verse shows, the elders are seated.
4:
On Revelation 4:4 — “And round about the throne were four and twenty seats: and upon the seats I saw four and twenty elders sitting, clothed in white raiment; and they had on their heads crowns of gold” — the witnesses mark the four and twenty elders.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the symbol. Robertson: “four and twenty thrones” (thronoi). Adam Clarke: perhaps the twenty-four courses of priests, or a reference to the lesser Sanhedrin. Ellicott: their pre-eminence. Wesley: the most holy of all former ages, representing the whole body of the saints.

The verse sets the elders around the throne. Twenty-four crowned elders in white sit enthroned about God. They represent the redeemed church glorified.

So the witnesses see, ringed about the central throne, the four and twenty enthroned elders. The witnesses note the inner circle: “round about the throne were four and twenty seats” (or thrones) — lesser thrones encircling the great one, on which sat “four and twenty elders.” They mark the common interpretation: the elders represent the redeemed church glorified — the number twenty-four often taken as the twelve patriarchs of Israel joined to the twelve apostles, the saints of the old and new covenants together, or (as Clarke suggests) recalling the twenty-four courses of priests who ministered in the temple. They observe their royal-priestly dignity: “clothed in white raiment” — the white robes of purity and victory; “and they had on their heads crowns of gold” — the golden crowns of those who have overcome and now reign. They note that the saints are pictured nearest the throne, enthroned and crowned, sharing in some measure the sovereignty of God — the very reward promised to the overcomers in the letters. They observe Wesley’s sense that these are the most holy of all former ages, representing the whole body of the redeemed. They note that the placement of the glorified saints around the throne, before the angels are even mentioned, marks the high honour of the redeemed. The verse thus surrounds the throne with the crowned elders: the church glorified, in white and gold, enthroned about God — who, as the next verse shows, reigns amid lightnings and the seven Spirits.
5:
On Revelation 4:5 — “And out of the throne proceeded lightnings and thunderings and voices: and there were seven lamps of fire burning before the throne, which are the seven Spirits of God” — the witnesses mark the majesty of the throne and the sevenfold Spirit.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the imagery. Robertson and Barnes: the lightnings, voices, and thunders recall Sinai (Exodus 19:16; Ezekiel 1:13), tokens of majesty. Adam Clarke: the seven lamps the seven angels. Wesley: the lightnings affect the sight, the voices the hearing, the thunders make the body tremble.

The verse displays the throne’s awful majesty. From it come lightnings, thunders, and voices; before it burn the seven Spirits as lamps. Power and the Spirit attend God’s throne.

So the witnesses see the throne attended by tokens of majesty and the sevenfold Spirit. The witnesses note the awe-inspiring phenomena: “out of the throne proceeded lightnings and thunderings and voices” — the same signs that marked God’s descent on Sinai, expressing the terrible majesty, power, and holiness of the Enthroned; as Wesley observes, the lightnings strike the sight, the voices the hearing, the thunders make the body tremble. They mark that these issue “out of the throne” itself: God’s power and his coming judgments proceed from where he reigns. They observe the seven lamps: “seven lamps of fire burning before the throne” — and the inspired interpretation given, “which are the seven Spirits of God,” understood as the Holy Spirit in the fulness and perfection of his operations (the “seven” denoting completeness), present before the throne as light and fire. They note that the same sevenfold Spirit appeared in the salutation and the letters; here he burns as lamps of fire, the perfect Spirit of God in active power. They observe the union in the scene: the throne of majesty, the signs of overwhelming power, and the fulness of the Spirit together. They note that the believer may take comfort that what to weak men seems terrible is to heaven a source of joy and worship. The verse thus surrounds the throne with majesty and the Spirit: lightnings and thunders of power, and the seven lamps of the sevenfold Spirit burning before it — with the crystal sea and the living creatures, the next verse adds.
6:
On Revelation 4:6 — “And before the throne there was a sea of glass like unto crystal: and in the midst of the throne… were four beasts full of eyes before and behind” — the witnesses mark the crystal sea and the living creatures.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the symbols. Robertson: “a glassy sea” (thalassa hyaline), the appearance, not the material. Adam Clarke: “beasts” is an improper rendering; rather “living creatures” (zoa). Ellicott: the sea before the throne. Wesley: a sea wide and deep, pure, transparent, and still; the eyes denoting wisdom and vigilance.

The verse adds the crystal sea and the four living creatures. Before the throne lies a glassy sea; about it are four living creatures full of eyes. Purity and ceaseless watchfulness attend God.

So the witnesses see, before the throne, a sea of glass, and around it the four living creatures. The witnesses note the sea: “a sea of glass like unto crystal” — a wide, calm, transparent expanse before the throne, clear and still as crystal; the expositors variously take it as an emblem of the pure, settled peace and holiness of God’s presence, or of the cleansing through which the redeemed approach. They mark the correction the commentators urge on the rendering “beasts”: the word means “living creatures” (zoa), not brute beasts; the term “beasts” is an unhappy translation. They observe these four living creatures, drawn from Ezekiel’s and Isaiah’s visions, stationed about the throne, in closest attendance on God. They note their being “full of eyes before and behind” — covered with eyes, the emblem of perfect knowledge, ceaseless vigilance, and all-seeing intelligence; nothing escapes them. They observe the range of interpretation as to what the four represent — angelic ministers, the cherubim, the redeemed in their highest service, or the powers of animate creation in God’s service; the expositors differ, but agree they are exalted beings nearest the throne, worshipping and serving. They note that the eyes mark them as instruments of God’s all-seeing providence. The verse thus sets before the throne the crystal sea and the eye-covered living creatures — whose form and unceasing praise the next verses unfold.
7:
On Revelation 4:7 — “And the first beast was like a lion, and the second beast like a calf, and the third beast had a face as a man, and the fourth beast was like a flying eagle” — the witnesses mark the four faces of the living creatures.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the four forms. Robertson (citing Swete): “whatever is noblest, strongest, wisest, and swiftest in nature.” Adam Clarke: a reference to the four standards of the tribes of Israel (lion, ox, man, eagle). Ellicott compares Ezekiel 1:10. Wesley: like the cherubim of Ezekiel.

The verse describes the four living creatures. They are like a lion, a calf, a man, and a flying eagle. Their forms embody the highest in creation.

So the witnesses see the four living creatures distinguished by four faces, drawn from Ezekiel’s cherubim. The witnesses note the four forms: “like a lion” — the king of beasts, emblem of strength and majesty; “like a calf” (or ox) — the strong patient laborer and the sacrificial victim; “a face as a man” — emblem of reason, wisdom, and the highest intelligence; and “like a flying eagle” — emblem of swiftness, soaring height, and keen sight. They mark Swete’s often-cited summary, that the four represent whatever is noblest, strongest, wisest, and swiftest in animate creation — the cream and crown of created life, gathered before the throne. They observe the connection the expositors draw with Ezekiel’s living creatures, where each cherub had all four faces, and with the four standards under which the tribes of Israel camped (lion of Judah, ox, man, eagle). They note that the church has from early times also seen in the four the emblems of the four Evangelists, though the text itself does not assign them. They observe that whatever the precise reference, the four faces express the fulness of created excellence rendering homage to God. They note that the noblest of creatures stand nearest the throne in ceaseless service. The verse thus pictures the four living creatures in their four forms — lion, ox, man, eagle — the summit of creation about the throne, whose unresting worship the next verse records.
8:
On Revelation 4:8 — “And the four beasts had each of them six wings about him; and they were full of eyes within: and they rest not day and night, saying, Holy, holy, holy, Lord God Almighty, which was, and is, and is to come” — the witnesses mark the unceasing song of the living creatures.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the seraphim. Robertson and Ellicott: six wings as the seraphim of Isaiah 6:2; teeming with eyes. The Puritan Henry: now the songs heard in heaven. Wesley: two wings cover the face in reverence, two the feet, two for ready flight; the threefold “Holy” as in Isaiah.

The verse gives the creatures’ ceaseless praise. Six-winged and full of eyes, they cry without rest, “Holy, holy, holy.” Heaven’s worship adores the eternal, almighty God.

So the witnesses see the four living creatures in unresting worship of the thrice-holy God. The witnesses note their form, drawn from Isaiah’s seraphim: “six wings about him” — with which, as Wesley recalls from Isaiah, they cover the face in reverence, cover the feet in humility, and fly in ready service; and “full of eyes within” — even beneath the wings teeming with eyes, all knowledge and vigilance. They mark the ceaselessness of their praise: “they rest not day and night” — their worship never pauses; adoration is the unbroken occupation of heaven. They observe the song: “Holy, holy, holy, Lord God Almighty” — the threefold ascription of holiness, the very anthem Isaiah heard, exalting the supreme, transcendent purity of God; the threefold repetition stresses the infinite holiness, and the church has heard in it a hint of the Trinity. They note the divine names that follow: “Lord God Almighty” — sovereign, omnipotent; and “which was, and is, and is to come” — the eternal One, unchanging through all time, the same titles given at the book’s opening. They observe that the highest creatures, nearest the throne, find their endless joy in adoring God’s holiness and eternity. They note that this song sets the keynote of heaven’s worship. The verse thus lifts the unceasing anthem of the living creatures: “Holy, holy, holy” to the eternal, almighty God — praise that moves, in the next verses, the elders to fall down and worship.
9:
On Revelation 4:9 — “And when those beasts give glory and honour and thanks to him that sat on the throne, who liveth for ever and ever” — the witnesses mark the creatures leading the worship.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the cue. Robertson: indefinite temporal clause with the future, the notion of repetition. Barnes: as often as the living creatures ascribe glory. The Puritan Henry: thus rise the forever harmonious, thankful songs of the redeemed. Wesley: as often as the living creatures give glory, immediately the elders fall down.

The verse marks the creatures’ lead in praise. Whenever the living creatures give glory to the eternal God, the elders respond. Heaven’s worship moves in ordered antiphon.

So the witnesses see the worship of heaven flow in ordered sequence, the living creatures leading and the elders following. The witnesses note the cue: “when those beasts give glory and honour and thanks” — the construction, as Robertson observes, denotes repeated, recurring action; as often as the four living creatures lift their ascription of praise, the response of the elders follows. They mark the threefold tribute the creatures render: “glory and honour and thanks” — ascribing to God the glory due his majesty, the honour due his name, and thanks for all his goodness. They observe the object: “him that sat on the throne, who liveth for ever and ever” — the eternal, ever-living God, whose endless being is the ground of endless praise. They note the pattern of heavenly worship the expositors draw out: the living creatures, nearest the throne, begin and lead; the elders, the redeemed church, immediately join and respond; worship rises in harmonious antiphon, the whole company moving together. They observe Henry’s point that thus rise the forever harmonious, thankful songs of the redeemed in heaven. They note that the praise is not occasional but the constant, ordered occupation of heaven, ever renewed. They observe that the eternity of God calls forth perpetual adoration. The verse thus shows the living creatures leading the worship of the eternal God — at which, the next verse declares, the elders fall down and cast their crowns before the throne.
10:
On Revelation 4:10 — “The four and twenty elders fall down before him… and worship him that liveth for ever and ever, and cast their crowns before the throne” — the witnesses mark the elders’ act of homage.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the homage. Robertson: the future tenses — fall down, worship, cast their crowns. Adam Clarke: an allusion to prostration in the east, and petty kings casting their crowns before a superior, acknowledging his supremacy. The elders own that their crowns are from God alone.

The verse gives the elders’ worship. They fall down, worship the eternal God, and cast their crowns before the throne. The crowned saints lay all honour at God’s feet.

So the witnesses see the redeemed elders answer the creatures’ praise with deepest homage. The witnesses note the prostration: “the four and twenty elders fall down before him that sat on the throne” — the glorified saints, crowned and enthroned themselves, cast themselves down in adoration before the supreme God; the highest of the redeemed bow lowest before him. They mark the object and ground: “and worship him that liveth for ever and ever” — they adore the eternal, ever-living God, echoing the creatures’ ascription. They observe the striking act: “and cast their crowns before the throne” — the elders take the golden crowns from their heads and lay them at God’s feet; Clarke notes the custom of subject kings casting their crowns before a sovereign in token of submission. They note the meaning the expositors draw: the saints acknowledge that all their honour, dignity, and reward come from God alone, not from themselves; they receive crowns, but render them back to him who gave them, claiming no glory for their own. They observe the humility of heaven: the more exalted the redeemed, the more they ascribe all to God. They note that this is the perfection of worship — the crowned bowing and surrendering their crowns. They observe that the gesture confesses God the source of every honour the saints possess. The verse thus pictures the elders’ homage: falling down, worshipping the eternal God, and casting their crowns before the throne — the act that frames their song of worthiness in the next verse.
11:
On Revelation 4:11 — “Thou art worthy, O Lord, to receive glory and honour and power: for thou hast created all things, and for thy pleasure they are and were created” — the witnesses mark the song of the Creator’s worthiness.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the doxology. Robertson and Ellicott: the threefold ascription — “our Lord and our God” (cf. John 20:28); worthy “because thou didst create all things.” Adam Clarke: he made all things for his pleasure, and through the same preserves. Wesley: he receives this not only when praised, but when he glorifies himself anew.

The verse gives the elders’ song. God is worthy of glory, honour, and power, because he created all things for his pleasure. Creation is the ground of his praise.

So the witnesses see the elders voice the ground of all worship: God’s worthiness as Creator. The witnesses note the ascription: “thou art worthy, O Lord” (the best texts, “our Lord and our God”) “to receive glory and honour and power” — the threefold tribute owned to be God’s due; he is fit and deserving to receive all the praise heaven can render. They mark the reason given: “for thou hast created all things” — the worthiness rests on creation; as the Maker of all that exists, God deserves the homage of all. They observe the further ground: “and for thy pleasure they are and were created” — all things owe both their original creation and their continued existence to God’s sovereign will and good pleasure; he made them because he willed, and they subsist by the same will. They note the theology the expositors draw: God is the absolute origin and end of all things; creation exists from him, by him, and for him, for his glory and delight. They observe that this song of the Creator’s worthiness fittingly opens the heavenly worship and prepares for the song of redemption in the next chapter: first God is praised as Creator, then the Lamb as Redeemer. They note that the casting down of the crowns is the act, and this song the word, of the same homage. The verse thus crowns the throne-vision with the elders’ song: God is worthy of glory, honour, and power as the Creator of all things for his pleasure — before the sealed book and the worthy Lamb fill the next chapter.
